question: John’s Prologue calls the Word both ‘with God’ and ‘God.’ How do those two phrases simultaneously block (a) modalism and (b) ‘Jesus is a created being’ arguments?

choices.A: ‘With God’ implies personal distinction; ‘God’ attributes true divinity—together excluding both one-person modalism and creature-only readings.
choices.B: They contradict each other, proving John is confused.
choices.C: They mean the Word is merely an angel near God.
choices.D: They teach God changed into the Word and stopped being God.

teaching.title: Distinction without division
teaching.body: John 1 holds two truths together: the Word is personally distinct (‘with God’) and truly divine (‘God’). That combination is precisely what later Trinitarian theology articulates: distinction of persons without denying unity of essence. It’s a textual roadblock to both modalism and ‘created Word’ claims.
